Meaning: Matches any single character except a newline by default
Use Case: Match one unknown character in a fixed pattern
Notes: With DOTALL mode it can also match newlines
Example Problem: Find a three-letter pattern starting with a and ending with c

Meaning: Matches one character from a specified set
Use Case: Match one character from several allowed options
Notes: Inside brackets most metacharacters lose their special meaning
Example Problem: Match the first vowel

Meaning: Matches one character not in the specified set
Use Case: Exclude specific characters from a match
Notes: ^ negates only when it appears at the start inside brackets
Example Problem: Match the first non-digit character

Meaning: Defines a range inside a character class
Use Case: Match characters within a continuous span of values
Notes: Usually special only inside brackets
Example Problem: Match the first lowercase letter from a mixed string

Meaning: Matches the start of the string
Use Case: Require a pattern to appear at the beginning
Notes: In MULTILINE mode it also matches the start of each line
Example Problem: Check whether a string starts with ID

Meaning: Matches the end of the string
Use Case: Require a pattern to appear at the end
Notes: In MULTILINE mode it also matches before each line break
Example Problem: Check whether a filename ends with .csv

Meaning: Matches only the absolute start of the string
Use Case: Anchor a pattern to the true beginning
Notes: Unlike ^ it is not affected by MULTILINE mode
Example Problem: Match a greeting only at the very beginning

Meaning: Matches only the absolute end of the string
Use Case: Anchor a pattern to the true end
Notes: Unlike $ it is not affected by MULTILINE mode
Example Problem: Match DONE only if it appears at the very end

Meaning: Matches a word boundary
Use Case: Match a whole word without matching it inside another word
Notes: A word boundary is the position between word and non-word characters
Example Problem: Find the standalone word cat

Meaning: Matches a position that is not a word boundary
Use Case: Match text only when it appears inside a larger word
Notes: Useful when you want to avoid whole-word matches
Example Problem: Find cat only when embedded inside another word

Meaning: Matches any digit character
Use Case: Find numbers in text
Notes: Equivalent to [0-9] in ASCII-style usage
Example Problem: Extract the first number from a string

Meaning: Matches any non-digit character
Use Case: Find the first character that is not a number
Notes: Opposite of \d
Example Problem: Extract the first non-digit after a numeric prefix

Meaning: Matches a word character such as letters digits or underscore
Use Case: Match identifiers or simple word-like tokens
Notes: Commonly treated like [A-Za-z0-9_] for many examples
Example Problem: Extract the first username-like token

Meaning: Matches a non-word character
Use Case: Find punctuation or separators
Notes: Opposite of \w
Example Problem: Extract the first punctuation mark

Meaning: Matches a whitespace character
Use Case: Find spaces tabs or newlines
Notes: Useful for flexible spacing in patterns
Example Problem: Extract the first run of whitespace

Meaning: Matches a non-whitespace character
Use Case: Find visible text while skipping spaces
Notes: Opposite of \s
Example Problem: Extract the first non-space token

Meaning: Matches zero or more repetitions of the preceding pattern
Use Case: Allow a pattern to repeat any number of times
Notes: Greedy by default
Example Problem: Match repeated digits at the start of a string

Meaning: Matches one or more repetitions of the preceding pattern
Use Case: Require at least one occurrence
Notes: Greedy by default
Example Problem: Match a full run of digits

Meaning: Makes the preceding pattern optional or marks a quantifier as lazy in combined forms
Use Case: Allow one optional character
Notes: By itself after a token it means zero or one occurrence
Example Problem: Match both color and colour

Meaning: Matches exactly n repetitions of the preceding pattern
Use Case: Require a fixed number of repeated characters
Notes: Works with single tokens or grouped patterns
Example Problem: Match exactly three digits

Meaning: Matches at least n repetitions of the preceding pattern
Use Case: Require a minimum repetition count
Notes: The match is greedy by default
Example Problem: Match a number with at least two digits

Meaning: Matches between n and m repetitions of the preceding pattern
Use Case: Allow a controlled repetition range
Notes: The match is greedy by default
Example Problem: Match a run of two to four digits

Meaning: Matches zero or more repetitions as few times as possible
Use Case: Make a star quantifier non-greedy
Notes: Useful for shortest-match extraction
Example Problem: Extract the first HTML-like tag only

Meaning: Matches one or more repetitions as few times as possible
Use Case: Make a plus quantifier non-greedy
Notes: Still requires at least one occurrence
Example Problem: Extract the shortest quoted string content with delimiters

Meaning: Makes an optional pattern lazy
Use Case: Prefer the shorter optional match
Notes: Less common than *? or +? but valid
Example Problem: Match the shorter optional u form

Meaning: Makes a bounded repetition lazy
Use Case: Prefer the smallest repetition count within the allowed range
Notes: Useful when a range would otherwise overmatch
Example Problem: Match the shortest run of two to four digits

Meaning: Groups part of a pattern and captures the matched text
Use Case: Extract a specific subpart of a larger match
Notes: Captured groups can be accessed with group numbers
Example Problem: Extract the area code from a phone number

Meaning: Groups part of a pattern without capturing it
Use Case: Group alternatives or repeated units without creating a numbered group
Notes: Useful when structure matters but you do not need to retrieve the group
Example Problem: Match repeated abc as one block

Meaning: Matches one alternative or another
Use Case: Match one of several possible patterns
Notes: Alternation is tried from left to right
Example Problem: Find either cat or dog in text
Input Example: import re; text = "I saw a dog today"

Meaning: Refers to the text captured by group 1
Use Case: Match repeated text using a previous capture
Notes: Backreferences depend on an earlier capturing group
Example Problem: Find a repeated word

Meaning: Creates a named capturing group
Use Case: Give a captured pattern a readable name
Notes: Named groups can still be accessed like normal groups
Example Problem: Extract a year using a named group

Meaning: Refers to a previously captured named group
Use Case: Match repeated text using a named backreference
Notes: Works like \1 but uses the group name
Example Problem: Find a repeated named word

Meaning: Asserts that a pattern follows without consuming it
Use Case: Require following context without including it in the match
Notes: This is a positive lookahead assertion
Example Problem: Match a number only if it is followed by USD

Meaning: Asserts that a pattern does not follow without consuming it
Use Case: Exclude matches based on following context
Notes: This is a negative lookahead assertion
Example Problem: Match cat only when it is not followed by s
Input Example: import re; text = "one cat two cats"

Syntax: {outer_key: {inner_key: inner_value for inner_item in inner_iterable} for outer_item in outer_iterable}
Meaning: Nested dict comprehension
Use Case: Build nested lookup mappings
Notes: Inner dict created per outer key
Example Answer: result = {col: {val: i for i, val in enumerate(vals)} for col, vals in data.items()}

Meaning: List comprehension
Use Case: Transform every item in a sequence into a new list
Notes: Expression comes before for
Example Problem:
## Convert the below to a list comprehension

result = []

for x in nums:
    result.append(x**2)

Syntax: [expr for item in iterable if condition]
Meaning: List comprehension with filter
Use Case: Filter items while creating a new list
Notes: If at end filters and has no else
Example Answer: result = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]

Syntax: [true_expr if condition else false_expr for item in iterable]
Meaning: List comprehension with if-else
Use Case: Replace or recode values while keeping list length
Notes: If-else goes before for
Example Answer: result = ['even' if x % 2 == 0 else 'odd' for x in nums]

Syntax: [expr for outer_item in outer_iterable for inner_item in outer_item]
Meaning: Nested list comprehension
Use Case: Flatten a nested list into one list
Notes: For clauses follow loop order
Example Answer: result = [num for row in matrix for num in row]

Syntax: [expr for outer_item in outer_iterable for inner_item in outer_item if condition]
Meaning: Nested list comprehension with filter
Use Case: Flatten nested data while filtering values
Notes: If applies to inner loop
Example Answer: result = [num for row in matrix for num in row if num > 0]

Syntax: [true_expr if condition else false_expr for outer_item in outer_iterable for inner_item in outer_item]
Meaning: Nested list comprehension with if-else
Use Case: Flatten nested data and replace values conditionally
Notes: If-else transforms values
Example Answer: result = [num if num > 0 else 0 for row in matrix for num in row]

Meaning: Dict comprehension
Use Case: Build a lookup dictionary from a sequence
Notes: Each loop creates key-value pair
Example Answer: result = {name: len(name) for name in names}

Syntax: {key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable if condition}
Meaning: Dict comprehension with filter
Use Case: Filter items while building a dictionary
Notes: If filters entries
Example Answer: result = {item: count for item, count in inventory.items() if count > 0}

Syntax: [(expr1, expr2) for item in iterable for inner_item in inner_iterable]
Meaning: Nested list comprehension creating tuples
Use Case: Keep context while flattening nested data
Notes: Outputs tuples
Example Answer: result = [(i, num) for i, row in enumerate(matrix) for num in row]

Syntax: [{'column': col, 'value': val, 'encoding': enc} for col, inner in mapping.items() for val, enc in inner.items()]
Meaning: Nested list comprehension creating dictionaries
Use Case: Flatten nested dictionary mappings into row-like records for tabular data
Notes: Each iteration produces one dictionary representing a row
Example Answer: result = [{'column': col, 'value': val, 'encoding': enc} for col, inner in mapping.items() for val, enc in inner.items()]

Meaning: Embeds expressions inside string literals using curly braces
Use Case: Format dynamic strings with variables
Notes: Preferred modern formatting syntax
Example Problem: I need to include a variable inside a string

Meaning: Defines a placeholder for substitution in a format string
Use Case: Mark positions where values will be inserted
Notes: Used with external formatting mechanisms
Example Problem: I need a placeholder for a variable in a string

Meaning: Specifies float precision to 2 decimal places
Use Case: Control decimal formatting in output
Notes: Only applies when formatting is executed
Example Problem: I need to display a float with 2 decimals

Meaning: Specifies comma separator for large numbers
Use Case: Improve readability of large numbers
Notes: Only applies when formatting is executed
Example Problem: I want commas in large numbers

Meaning: Right aligns content within width of 10
Use Case: Align text output in columns
Notes: Padding depends on formatting execution
Example Problem: I need right aligned text

Meaning: Left aligns content within width of 10
Use Case: Align text to left in fixed width
Notes: Padding applied during formatting
Example Problem: I need left aligned text

Meaning: Centers content within width of 10
Use Case: Center text in fixed width display
Notes: Padding applied during formatting
Example Problem: I want centered text

Meaning: Pads value with leading zeros to width 5
Use Case: Format numeric identifiers with leading zeros
Notes: Only applies during formatting
Example Problem: I want ID-like formatting

Meaning: Formats number as percentage with no decimals
Use Case: Display ratios as percentages
Notes: Requires numeric input during formatting
Example Problem: I want to show percent

Meaning: Concatenates two strings using plus operator
Use Case: Combine strings manually
Notes: Works only with string types
Example Problem: I want to combine two strings

Meaning: Defines raw string literal where backslashes are not escaped
Use Case: Work with regex or file paths
Notes: Backslashes are treated literally
Example Problem: I need to write a Windows path

Meaning: Represents newline character inside string
Use Case: Create multi-line strings
Notes: Escape sequence must be interpreted
Example Problem: I want a line break in a string

Meaning: Represents tab character inside string
Use Case: Indent or space text horizontally
Notes: Escape sequence must be interpreted
Example Problem: I want tab spacing

Meaning: Splits string into list based on delimiter
Use Case: Break string into parts
Notes: Returns list not string
Example Problem: I want to split words

Meaning: Replaces substring with another substring
Use Case: Modify parts of a string
Notes: Returns new string
Example Problem: I want to replace text

Meaning: Concatenates text values together.
Use Case: Combine columns or string pieces into one formatted value.
Notes: Returns NULL if one side is NULL unless handled with COALESCE().
Example Problem: I have first and last names in separate columns, and I need one full_name column.
Example Answer: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM customers;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers (first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT);\nINSERT INTO customers VALUES ('Mia', 'Chen');\nSELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM customers;

Meaning: Returns unique rows based on the selected expression or columns.
Use Case: Remove duplicate values from query results.
Notes: DISTINCT applies to the whole selected row, not just one column when multiple columns are selected.
Example Problem: I have repeated crime categories, and I need each category listed once.
Input Example: CREATE TABLE crimes (category TEXT);\nINSERT INTO crimes VALUES ('THEFT'), ('THEFT'), ('ROBBERY');\nSELECT DISTINCT category FROM crimes;

Meaning: Returns unique concatenated text values.
Use Case: Create unique formatted labels from multiple text pieces.
Notes: Concatenate first, then DISTINCT removes duplicate final strings.
Example Problem: I have repeated city and state values, and I need each location label once.
Example Answer: SELECT DISTINCT city || ', ' || state AS location FROM offices;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE offices (city TEXT, state TEXT);\nINSERT INTO offices VALUES ('Seattle', 'WA'), ('Seattle', 'WA'), ('Austin', 'TX');\nSELECT DISTINCT city || ', ' || state AS location FROM offices;

Meaning: Creates conditional output based on logical rules.
Use Case: Turn values into readable categories or labels.
Notes: Always close CASE expressions with END.
Example Problem: I have order totals, and I need to label each order as high or normal value.
Example Answer: SELECT order_id, CASE WHEN total >= 100 THEN 'high' ELSE 'normal' END AS value_label FROM orders;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders (order_id INTEGER, total INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO orders VALUES (1, 120), (2, 45);\nSELECT order_id, CASE WHEN total >= 100 THEN 'high' ELSE 'normal' END AS value_label FROM orders;

Meaning: Places conditional logic directly in the output columns.
Use Case: Create a calculated label while keeping the original rows.
Notes: This changes display output, not the stored table values.
Example Problem: I have account statuses coded as A and I, and I need readable labels in my result.
Example Answer: SELECT user_id, CASE WHEN status = 'A' THEN 'active' ELSE 'inactive' END AS status_label FROM users;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE users (user_id INTEGER, status TEXT);\nINSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'A'), (2, 'I');\nSELECT user_id, CASE WHEN status = 'A' THEN 'active' ELSE 'inactive' END AS status_label FROM users;

Meaning: Uses conditional logic to control custom sorting.
Use Case: Sort important categories first instead of alphabetical order.
Notes: Useful when normal ASC or DESC order is not meaningful enough.
Example Problem: I have ticket priorities, and I need urgent tickets to appear first.
Example Answer: SELECT ticket_id, priority FROM tickets ORDER BY CASE WHEN priority = 'urgent' THEN 0 ELSE 1 END, ticket_id;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE tickets (ticket_id INTEGER, priority TEXT);\nINSERT INTO tickets VALUES (1, 'normal'), (2, 'urgent'), (3, 'low');\nSELECT ticket_id, priority FROM tickets ORDER BY CASE WHEN priority = 'urgent' THEN 0 ELSE 1 END, ticket_id;

Meaning: Creates a temporary named result used by the main query.
Use Case: Break a query into readable steps before selecting from it.
Notes: The CTE only exists for that one query.
Example Problem: I have sales rows, and I want to calculate revenue first before filtering high-revenue rows.
Example Answer: WITH sales_calc AS (SELECT item, price * quantity AS revenue FROM sales) SELECT item, revenue FROM sales_calc WHERE revenue >= 100;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE sales (item TEXT, price INTEGER, quantity INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('Desk', 50, 3), ('Pen', 2, 10);\nWITH sales_calc AS (SELECT item, price * quantity AS revenue FROM sales) SELECT item, revenue FROM sales_calc WHERE revenue >= 100;

Meaning: Creates multiple temporary named results before the final query.
Use Case: Separate cleanup, aggregation, or formatting into clear query stages.
Notes: Separate CTEs with commas before the final SELECT.
Example Problem: I have daily sales, and I need to summarize totals after creating a cleaned sales step.
Example Answer: WITH clean_sales AS (SELECT store, amount FROM sales WHERE amount > 0), store_totals AS (SELECT store, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM clean_sales GROUP BY store) SELECT store, total_amount FROM store_totals;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE sales (store TEXT, amount INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('A', 100), ('A', 50), ('B', 0), ('B', 80);\nWITH clean_sales AS (SELECT store, amount FROM sales WHERE amount > 0), store_totals AS (SELECT store, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM clean_sales GROUP BY store) SELECT store, total_amount FROM store_totals;

Meaning: Gives a column or table expression an alias.
Use Case: Rename calculated outputs so results are easier to read.
Notes: Aliases can be used in ORDER BY and often in GROUP BY, but not reliably in WHERE.
Example Problem: I calculated revenue, and I need the output column name to say revenue.
Example Answer: SELECT item, price * quantity AS revenue FROM sales;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE sales (item TEXT, price INTEGER, quantity INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('Notebook', 5, 4);\nSELECT item, price * quantity AS revenue FROM sales;

Meaning: Combines rows that share the same grouping value.
Use Case: Summarize records by category, month, store, or user.
Notes: Every selected non-aggregated column should usually appear in GROUP BY.
Example Problem: I have orders by store, and I need total revenue per store.
Example Answer: SELECT store, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY store;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders (store TEXT, revenue INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO orders VALUES ('A', 100), ('A', 80), ('B', 50);\nSELECT store, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY store;

Meaning: Filters grouped results after aggregation.
Use Case: Keep only groups that meet an aggregate condition.
Notes: Use WHERE before grouping and HAVING after grouping.
Example Problem: I have orders by store, and I only want stores with total revenue at least 100.
Example Answer: SELECT store, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY store HAVING SUM(revenue) >= 100;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders (store TEXT, revenue INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO orders VALUES ('A', 100), ('A', 80), ('B', 50);\nSELECT store, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY store HAVING SUM(revenue) >= 100;

Meaning: Filters rows before grouping or aggregation.
Use Case: Keep only rows that meet a condition.
Notes: WHERE cannot directly filter aggregate results like SUM().
Example Problem: I have orders from multiple years, and I only want 2024 rows before summarizing.
Example Answer: SELECT store, revenue FROM orders WHERE order_year = 2024;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders (store TEXT, revenue INTEGER, order_year INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO orders VALUES ('A', 100, 2024), ('B', 80, 2023);\nSELECT store, revenue FROM orders WHERE order_year = 2024;

Meaning: Sorts query results by one or more expressions.
Use Case: Rank or arrange rows in a readable order.
Notes: Default sort is ascending unless DESC is specified.
Example Problem: I have product sales, and I need the highest revenue products first.
Example Answer: SELECT product, revenue FROM sales ORDER BY revenue DESC;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE sales (product TEXT, revenue INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('Keyboard', 90), ('Laptop', 1200), ('Mouse', 40);\nSELECT product, revenue FROM sales ORDER BY revenue DESC;

Meaning: Restricts how many rows a query returns.
Use Case: Preview data or return the top N rows after sorting.
Notes: Use ORDER BY with LIMIT when top rows need a meaningful order.
Example Problem: I have sales rows sorted by revenue, and I only want the top 2.
Example Answer: SELECT product, revenue FROM sales ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 2;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE sales (product TEXT, revenue INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('Keyboard', 90), ('Laptop', 1200), ('Mouse', 40);\nSELECT product, revenue FROM sales ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 2;

Meaning: Converts a value to an integer type.
Use Case: Turn numeric text into numbers for calculation or sorting.
Notes: Invalid numeric text may convert to 0 in SQLite.
Example Problem: I have month numbers stored as text, and I need to convert them to numbers for math.
Example Answer: SELECT month_text, CAST(month_text AS INTEGER) AS month_number FROM months;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE months (month_text TEXT);\nINSERT INTO months VALUES ('01'), ('12');\nSELECT month_text, CAST(month_text AS INTEGER) AS month_number FROM months;

Meaning: Combines rows from multiple SELECT statements and keeps duplicates.
Use Case: Append similar result sets when duplicates are meaningful.
Notes: Each SELECT must return the same number of columns in the same order.
Example Problem: I have online and store orders with the same columns, and I need one combined order list while keeping duplicate order amounts.
Example Answer: SELECT order_id, amount FROM online_orders UNION ALL SELECT order_id, amount FROM store_orders;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE online_orders(order_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO online_orders VALUES (1, 20), (2, 30); CREATE TABLE store_orders(order_id INTEGER, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO store_orders VALUES (3, 20), (4, 40);

Meaning: Combines rows from multiple SELECT statements and removes duplicate rows.
Use Case: Create one unique list from similar result sets.
Notes: Duplicate removal checks the full row, not just one column.
Example Problem: I have emails from customers and newsletter signups, and I need one unique email list.
Example Answer: SELECT email FROM customers UNION SELECT email FROM newsletter;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(email TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('a@test.com'), ('b@test.com'); CREATE TABLE newsletter(email TEXT); INSERT INTO newsletter VALUES ('b@test.com'), ('c@test.com');

Meaning: Returns only rows that appear in both SELECT results.
Use Case: Find overlap between two similar result sets.
Notes: Rows must match exactly across all selected columns.
Example Problem: I need to find emails that appear in both the customer table and the newsletter table.
Example Answer: SELECT email FROM customers INTERSECT SELECT email FROM newsletter;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(email TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('a@test.com'), ('b@test.com'); CREATE TABLE newsletter(email TEXT); INSERT INTO newsletter VALUES ('b@test.com'), ('c@test.com');

Meaning: Returns rows from the first SELECT result that do not appear in the second SELECT result.
Use Case: Find records in one result set but missing from another.
Notes: Order matters because A EXCEPT B is different from B EXCEPT A.
Example Problem: I need to find customers who are not subscribed to the newsletter.
Example Answer: SELECT email FROM customers EXCEPT SELECT email FROM newsletter;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(email TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('a@test.com'), ('b@test.com'); CREATE TABLE newsletter(email TEXT); INSERT INTO newsletter VALUES ('b@test.com'), ('c@test.com');

Meaning: Chooses columns from a table and returns matching rows.
Use Case: Retrieve specific columns instead of the whole table.
Notes: Use * only when you intentionally want every column.
Example Problem: I have an orders table and only need customer names and amounts.
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40);

Meaning: Filters rows before results are returned.
Use Case: Keep only rows that satisfy a condition.
Notes: WHERE runs before grouping and aggregation.
Example Problem: I have orders and only want rows where the amount is at least 30.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, amount FROM orders WHERE amount >= 30;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40);

Meaning: Sorts the final result set by one or more expressions.
Use Case: Display query results in a meaningful order.
Notes: Use ASC for ascending and DESC for descending.
Example Problem: I have orders and want the largest amounts first.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, amount FROM orders ORDER BY amount DESC;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40), ('Ava', 30);

Meaning: Restricts how many rows are returned.
Use Case: Return only the top few rows after sorting or filtering.
Notes: LIMIT is commonly paired with ORDER BY for top-N queries.
Example Problem: I have orders and want only the two largest orders.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, amount FROM orders ORDER BY amount DESC LIMIT 2;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40), ('Ava', 30);

Meaning: Removes duplicate rows from the selected result.
Use Case: Get unique values or unique row combinations.
Notes: DISTINCT applies to the full selected row.
Example Problem: I have repeated departments and need each department listed once.
Input Example: CREATE TABLE employees(name TEXT, department TEXT); INSERT INTO employees VALUES ('Mia', 'Sales'), ('Noah', 'Sales'), ('Ava', 'IT');

Meaning: Groups rows that share the same values so aggregate results can be calculated per group.
Use Case: Summarize rows by category.
Notes: Selected non-aggregated columns should be part of the grouping logic.
Example Problem: I have orders by customer and need each customer's total spending.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, SUM(amount) FROM orders GROUP BY customer;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Mia', 15), ('Noah', 40);

Meaning: Filters groups after GROUP BY has created aggregate results.
Use Case: Keep only groups that meet an aggregate condition.
Notes: Use WHERE for row-level filtering and HAVING for group-level filtering.
Example Problem: I need customers whose total order amount is at least 40.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, SUM(amount) FROM orders GROUP BY customer HAVING SUM(amount) >= 40;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Mia', 15), ('Noah', 30);

Meaning: Combines rows from two tables when the join condition matches.
Use Case: Add related columns from another table.
Notes: Only matching rows from both tables are returned.
Example Problem: I have orders with customer IDs and need customer names attached.
Example Answer: SELECT orders.order_id, customers.name FROM orders INNER JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(customer_id INTEGER, name TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Mia'), (2, 'Noah'); CREATE TABLE orders(order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (101, 1), (102, 3);

Meaning: Keeps all rows from the left table and adds matching rows from the right table when available.
Use Case: Find records even when related details may be missing.
Notes: Unmatched right-table columns return NULL.
Example Problem: I have customers and want to see their orders, including customers with no orders.
Example Answer: SELECT customers.name, orders.order_id FROM customers LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(customer_id INTEGER, name TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Mia'), (2, 'Noah'); CREATE TABLE orders(order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (101, 1);

Meaning: Defines the condition used to match rows between joined tables.
Use Case: Match related rows using key columns.
Notes: Incorrect ON conditions can multiply rows or match unrelated records.
Example Problem: I need to join orders to customers using customer_id, not by customer name text.
Example Answer: SELECT orders.order_id, customers.name FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(customer_id INTEGER, name TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1, 'Mia'); CREATE TABLE orders(order_id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES (101, 1);

Meaning: Gives a temporary name to a column, expression, or table in a query result.
Use Case: Make output labels or table references easier to read.
Notes: The alias exists only for the query result.
Example Problem: I calculated total spending and want the output column named total_spent.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Mia', 15);

Meaning: Creates conditional values based on logical tests.
Use Case: Label or categorize rows inside a query.
Notes: CASE returns one value per row or group depending on where it is used.
Example Problem: I need to label orders as large if the amount is at least 30 and small otherwise.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, CASE WHEN amount >= 30 THEN 'large' ELSE 'small' END AS size_label FROM orders;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40);

Meaning: Checks whether a value matches any value in a list or subquery.
Use Case: Filter rows against multiple allowed values.
Notes: IN is cleaner than several OR comparisons.
Example Problem: I need orders only from Mia or Ava.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, amount FROM orders WHERE customer IN ('Mia', 'Ava');
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40), ('Ava', 30);

Meaning: Checks whether a value falls within an inclusive range.
Use Case: Filter numeric or date-like values between two bounds.
Notes: BETWEEN includes both endpoints.
Example Problem: I need orders with amounts from 25 through 40.
Example Answer: SELECT customer, amount FROM orders WHERE amount BETWEEN 25 AND 40;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE orders(customer TEXT, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO orders VALUES ('Mia', 25), ('Noah', 40), ('Ava', 50);

Meaning: Compares text to a pattern using wildcards.
Use Case: Filter text values by prefix, suffix, or contained characters.
Notes: % means any number of characters and _ means one character.
Example Problem: I need customer names that start with M.
Example Answer: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE name LIKE 'M%';
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(name TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('Mia'), ('Noah'), ('Maya');

Meaning: Checks whether a value is NULL.
Use Case: Find missing values in a table.
Notes: Use IS NULL instead of = NULL.
Example Problem: I need customers who do not have an email saved.
Example Answer: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE email IS NULL;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(name TEXT, email TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('Mia', NULL), ('Noah', 'noah@test.com');

Meaning: Checks whether a value is not NULL.
Use Case: Keep only rows with present values.
Notes: Use IS NOT NULL instead of != NULL.
Example Problem: I need customers who have an email saved.
Example Answer: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE email IS NOT NULL;
Input Example: CREATE TABLE customers(name TEXT, email TEXT); INSERT INTO customers VALUES ('Mia', NULL), ('Noah', 'noah@test.com');
